coredata.dc:title: Chemiluminescence of doped carbon dots with H2O2-KMnO4 system for the detection of Cu2+ and tannin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yaqiong
Jiang, Yamei
Wang, Xiaowei
Han, Suqin
coredata.dc:description: The carbon dots doped with chlorine and phosphorus (CDs-Cl,P) were used as chemiluminescence (CL) reagent for the sensitive detection of copper ions (Cu2+) and tannin (TA). The CDs-Cl,P was found to strongly enhance the reaction of H2O2 and KMnO4 in alkaline medium. The enhanced CL behavior of CDs-Cl,P was investigated and it was found that some radicals such as •OH, •O2 – and 1O2 appeared in the CL reaction process. The participation of Cu2+ could result in an enhanced CL intensity of the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system due to the Cu2+-catalyzed decomposition of H2O2 resulting in more •OH generation. Therefore, the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system was used to selectively quantify Cu2+ in solution by CL emission. A linear increase was observed between CL intensity and Cu2+ concentration. The CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system allowed the detection of Cu2+ down to lower concentration of 0.1 μM with a linear range of 0.2–60.0 μM. Moreover, TA as a common polyphenolic compound, could selectively decrease the CL signal of the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4-Cu2+ system due to its complexation with Cu2+. On this basis, the CL assay for TA was also developed. The detection limit was 0.14 μM and the linear range was from 5.0 μM to 100.0 μM. The proposed method was successfully applied to the determination of Cu2+ and TA in water, rice dumplings leaves, sodium copper chlorophyllin and wine samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Doped carbon dots
Cu2+
Tannin
Chemiluminescence

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the flow injection CL system. P1 and P2, peristaltic pumps; V, valve; HV, negative high voltage; PMT, photomultipier tube.

CL kinetic curves. 1: H2O2-KMnO4; 2: CDs-Cl,P-KMnO4; 3: CDs-Cl,P-H2O2; 4: CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4; 5: CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4-Cu2+; 6: CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4-Cu2+-TA. Inset: Injecting CDs-Cl,P into H2O2-KMnO4 (1), injecting KMnO4 into CDs-Cl,P-H2O2 (2) and injecting H2O2 into CDs-Cl,P-KMnO4 (3).

CL signal of CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system (black), CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system with Cu2+ or other species (red) and CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4-Cu2+ system with TA or other species (blue). The concentrations of all species were 1.0 μM.

(a) FL spectra of CDs-Cl,P-KMnO4 (1), CDs-Cl,P-H2O2 (2), CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 (3) and CDs-Cl,P (4) at 360 nm excitation wavelength. (b) CL spectra of CDs-Cl,P-H2O2 (1), CDs-Cl,P-KMnO4 (2), CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 (3), CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4-Cu2+-TA (4) and CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4-Cu2+ (5).
Schematic representation of CL for the detection of Cu2+ and TA.
The possible mechanism of CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 CL reaction.

head.ce:title: Chemiluminescence of doped carbon dots with H2O2-KMnO4 system for the detection of Cu2+ and tannin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041000, Shanxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041000, Shanxi, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The CDs-Cl,P was found to strongly enhance the reaction of H2O2 and KMnO4 in alkaline medium. The participation of Cu2+ could result in an enhanced CL intensity of the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system due to the Cu2+-catalyzed decomposition of H2O2. Moreover, TA could decrease the CL signal of the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4-Cu2+ system due to its complexation with Cu2+. Therefore, the CDs-Cl,P was used as CL reagent for the sensitive detection of Cu2+ and TA with satisfactory results.

The carbon dots doped with chlorine and phosphorus (CDs-Cl,P) were used as chemiluminescence (CL) reagent for the sensitive detection of copper ions (Cu2+) and tannin (TA). The CDs-Cl,P was found to strongly enhance the reaction of H2O2 and KMnO4 in alkaline medium. The enhanced CL behavior of CDs-Cl,P was investigated and it was found that some radicals such as •OH, •O2 – and 1O2 appeared in the CL reaction process. The participation of Cu2+ could result in an enhanced CL intensity of the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system due to the Cu2+-catalyzed decomposition of H2O2 resulting in more •OH generation. Therefore, the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system was used to selectively quantify Cu2+ in solution by CL emission. A linear increase was observed between CL intensity and Cu2+ concentration. The CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system allowed the detection of Cu2+ down to lower concentration of 0.1 μM with a linear range of 0.2–60.0 μM. Moreover, TA as a common polyphenolic compound, could selectively decrease the CL signal of the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4-Cu2+ system due to its complexation with Cu2+. On this basis, the CL assay for TA was also developed. The detection limit was 0.14 μM and the linear range was from 5.0 μM to 100.0 μM. The proposed method was successfully applied to the determination of Cu2+ and TA in water, rice dumplings leaves, sodium copper chlorophyllin and wine samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is one of the most important trace elements and is responsible for various physiological functions in human body [1]. It is an important component of proteins and enzymes such as ceruloplasmin and cytochrome c oxidase and plays an important role in many biological processes such as energy production, oxygen transport and activation, signal transduction and enzyme activation [2,3]. However, dysregulation of copper may contribute to neurodegenerative disorders such as Menkes disease and Wilson’s disease [4]. In view of the significance of copper, development of simple and rapid detection method to monitor Cu2+ level in environmental and food samples is necessary and essential. Tannin (TA) is a natural plant polyphenol that occurs in the seed, stem and leave of plants. It exhibits anticarcinogenic, antimicrobial, antioxidant, and antitoxic activities [5]. TA is commonly used as a mordant and flavoring agent in the printing and dyeing industry and in food industry. However, the release of TA into the environment can cause water contaminations and be bad for public health. For example, TA is toxic to aquatic species such as fish, phytoplankton and algae, and various chlorinated disinfection byproducts can be formed from TA [6,7]. Ingestion of TA may cause hardening of the gastrointestinal mucosa. Therefore, a simple and valid method for detecting TA is certainly very useful and of great important. So far, many methods have been developed for the detection Cu2+ and TA, such as colorimetric method [8–10], fluorescence (FL) method [11–15], plasmonic spectroscopy [16,17], electrochemistry [18–20], chemiluminescence (CL) and electrochemiluminescence [21–25].

CL is generally generated by oxidation reactions, and the light is emitted immediately when a chemical reaction occurs between reaction reagents. The driving energy of CL reaction is provided by the chemical reaction without any additional light source, therefore CL method is a fast and sensitive detection method. So far, the CL method has attracted considerable interest for its potential applications in chemical detection, bioanalysis, and food analysis due to the advantages of a simple instrument and environmental friendliness [26,27].

Carbon dots (CDs), a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have high fluorescence quantum yield, low toxicity, good photostability, tunable emission wavelength and are easily functionalized. They are highly valued by researchers and are used in many fields [28–30] because their synthesis methods are simple, easy and environmentally friendly and because the raw materials for synthesis are abundant and inexpensive. Due to their special physicochemical properties, CDs can participate in the CL reaction as oxidants, emitting species, energy acceptors of chemical reaction energy, or even catalysis in various CL systems. These novel influences on the CL reaction have led to the widespread use of these CL systems in conjunction with CDs in analytical chemistry [27,28].

CL signals were obtained using a model IFIS-C flow injection (Xi’an Remax Analytical Instrument Co. Ltd., Shannxi, China) and a BPCL ultra-weak CL analyzer (Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained using a JEOL JEM-2100 TEM (Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was analyzed using a VARIAN 660-IR spectrometer (Varian, USA). The UV–vis absorption spectrum was measured using a TU-1810 PC spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectrum was recorded using Ultima IV-185 X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). FL experiments were performed using an F-380 FL spectrophotometer (Tianjin Gangdong Science and Technology Development Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded using a K-Alpha surface analysis system (Thermo, USA).

All reagents in the experiments were of analytical reagent grade. H2O2, KMnO4, sucrose, CuSO4, HCl and H3PO4 were supplied by Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technologies (Tianjin, China). TA was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). CDs-Cl,P was prepared by the hydrothermal method and characterized by UV–vis spectroscopy, FL spectra, XRD, TEM, FI-TR and XPS (Fig. S1, S2). Deionized water was applied throughout the experiments. 1.0 M H2O2 solution was prepared using 30% H2O2 regent solution. 1.0 mM KMnO4, CuSO4 and TA solutions were prepared with their solid regents, respectively. The prepared CDs-Cl,P solution was suitable diluted before use.

Fig. 1 showed the schematic diagrams of the manifold used in this work. Cu2+ detection was performed according to route 1, and TA detection was completed according to route 2. The CL signal was acquired by a photomultiplier tube (PMT) equipped with the flow injection CL analyzer at a high voltage of –600 V and imported into the computer for data acquisition.

CL experiments were performed under constant conditions. For Cu2+ detection, the CL response was expressed as ΔI = I – I 0, where I 0 and I showed the CL intensity in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively. For TA detection, the CL response was expressed as ΔI = I 0 – I, where I 0 and I indicated the CL intensity in the absence and presence of TA, respectively.

The Cu2+ concentration in some real samples, including rice dumpling leaves, sodium copper chlorophyllin and mineral water, was determined, and the TA content in wine was analyzed. The mineral water sample was used directly and the wine sample was used after 10-fold dilution. The samples of rice dumpling leaves and sodium copper chlorophyllin had to be burned in high-temperature furnace before determination. The sample of rice dumpling leaves was washed three times with deionized water, dried by airing and homogenized in a blender. Ten crucibles were used, five of which were filled with 3.0 g rice dumpling leaves homogenate samples and the rest with 0.5 g sodium copper chlorophyllin samples. The samples of rice dumpling leaves were carbonized in the electric furnace before being ashed in the high-temperature furnace. Then, ten crucibles were placed in a high temperature furnace and fired at 500 ◦C for 1 h. After cooling to room temperature, the residue in each crucible was soaked with 20 mL of deionized water and filtered, respectively. The filtrate was diluted to 50 mL with water and used for the determination of Cu2+.

Preliminary experiments as shown in Fig. 2 were conducted for the design of the CL system. The reaction between H2O2 and KMnO4 in alkaline medium did not result in any significant CL emission, probably it’s because the O2 from the reaction of H2O2-KMnO4 was in the form of 3O2 [32]. When CDs-Cl,P was added into this reaction, significant CL emission was observed, peaking within 1.5 s. When CDs-Cl,P was mixed with the KMnO4, there was no CL emission, and a weak CL signal was obtained when CDs-Cl,P was mixed H2O2. In general, the way of mixing the reagent plays a key role in the CL reaction and is the basis for designing the CL flow system. The effect of the mixing order of reagent solutions on the CL signal was studied. The largest signal was observed when H2O2 was injected into the mixture of CDs-Cl,P and KMnO4 (inset of Fig. 2). Meanwhile, the CL behavior of CDs-Cl,P, CDs-N, CDs-Cl, CDs-P and bare CDs in H2O2-KMnO4 system were compared (Fig. S3a). All five CDs were found to enhance the CL intensity in this system, among the five, CDs-Cl,P showed the greatest enhancement. Fig. 2 also indicated that the CL signal of the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system was significantly enhanced by addition of Cu2+, and that the enhanced CL signal was quenched by TA. In Fig. 3 , the effect of some metal ions and compounds at the concentrations of 1.0 μM for the CL intensity of the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system was studied. The result showed that Cu2+ had a significant sensitizing effect and the influence of other species could be ignored. Furthermore, only TA had a significant inhibitory effect on the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4-Cu2+ system. Based on these experiment phenomena, a flow injection CL system was designed and was used for the determination of Cu2+ and TA as shown in Fig. 1.

It is well-know that H2O2 decomposition can be accelerated by some transition metal according to the Fenton-like mechanism [33]. In Fig. 3, Cu2+ significantly enhanced the CL intensity of the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system, Fe2+, Co2+ and other metal ions exhibited a weaker effect, consistent with their catalytic ability in Fenton or Fenton-like reactions [34–36]. Under acidic condition, Fe2+ actually exhibited a relatively higher catalytic activity toward H2O2 decomposition compared to Cu2+ (Fig. S3b). However, a reduced catalytic activity of Fe2+ in neutral solutions was observed, as a result of the pH dependence when Fe2+ catalyzed Fenton reaction [37,38]. In contrast, the catalytic activity of Cu2+ increased with increasing pH [37]. Moreover, Cu2+ exhibited a faster radical production rate under the CL assaying condition than in the presence of an equivalent Fe2+ addition [37]. The production rates of radical species originating from Cu2+ catalyzed Fenton-like reactions was higher than that of Co2+, because the catalytic activity compared to Fenton-like reactions induced by Cu2+ was better than Co2+ under alkaline condition [39]. Therefore, in comparison with Fe2+ and Co2+, Cu2+ induced the largest CL enhancement for the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 CL system. Other investigated metal ions as Fe3+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, and Hg2+ had little effect on the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 CL system. That is, Cu2+ exhibited excellent specificity enhancement for the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 CL system, which will be beneficial for CL application.

Previous studies in copper-catalyzed Fenton-like reaction have shown that Cu2+ can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to reactive compounds that increase the production rate of •OH and •O2 – [40,41]. The mechanism of Cu2+-enhanced H2O2-induced CL has been investigated in recent year [42,43]. For the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system, Cu2+ could catalyze H2O2 to generate •OH and •O2 – similar to Fenton reaction, which accelerated the production of reactive oxygen species. Table S1 showed that when the same amount scavengers was added to the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system and the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4-Cu2+ system, the inhibition rate of the latter was lower than that of the former, which was due to the fact that more free radicals formed in the presence of Cu2+ in the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system. The free radicals formed could interact with each other and/or with CDs-Cl,P. Therefore, a significant enhancement of the CL signal was observed. Thus, Cu2+ catalyzed H2O2 to release oxygen-associated free radical, which reacted with CDs-Cl,P and accelerated electron-hole annihilation to release energy in the form of CL emission. The kinetic results in Fig. 2 curve 5 showed that the addition of Cu2+ to the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 CL system led to an increase in the CL signal in 1.0 s. The rapid response was highly desirable for analytical detection and was one of the prerequisites for establishing a flow injection system.

The effects of the concentration of CDs-Cl,P, KMnO4, H2O2 and NaOH on the CL analysis were investigated (Fig. S4). The results showed that 1:20 CDs-Cl,P/H2O2 (v/v), 0.5 mM KMnO4, 0.3 M H2O2 and 0.04 M NaOH was employed as the implementing optimum condition for the detection of Cu2+. The CL intensity increased dramatically with the addition of 0.2 μM to 60.0 μM Cu2+ in flow injection system (Fig. S5a). Fig. S5b showed the relationship between the CL intensity of the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system and the Cu2+ concentration, where the CL intensity increased exponentially with the Cu2+ concentration increasing. The linear equation was ΔI = 120.6 + 369.7 lg[Cu2+] (μM) with a correlation coefficient (r) of 0.9928. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+ was 0.10 µM (3σ). The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 1.2% for eleven replicated analyses of 1.0 μM Cu2+, showing a good precision of this method for the determination of divalent copper ion.

To investigate the effect of CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 CL system on the specificity of Cu2+ determination, the developed CL method was applied for the analysis of 1.0 μM Cu2+ in the presence of some other compounds (including compounds present in real samples and common species listed in Fig. S4c). It became clear that reasonable selectivity was achieved for the determination of Cu2+. Consequently, the developed CL system could improve the selectivity of the detection method for Cu2+.

To investigate the applicability of the CL system, it was used for the determination of Cu2+ in real samples. The analyses were repeated after certain amounts of Cu2+ standard solutions were added to the samples before they were prepared. The results and the recoveries were shown in Table S2. It could be found that the Cu2+ recovery ranged from 96% to 105%, and the corresponding RSD ranged form 2.10% to 6.81%.

The CL signal of the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4-Cu2+ system was decreased by the addition of TA. The reason could be the formation of stable complexes of Cu2+-TA from the coordination between Cu2+ and the hydroxyl groups of TA [31]. A shift of the absorption peak of TA from 278 nm to 325 nm in the presence of Cu2+ (Fig. S6a) represented a decrease in the concentration of free Cu2+, which led to a decrease in CL.

To evaluate the analytical performance of the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4-Cu2+ system, TA was added to the detection solution at various concentrations under optimal conditions including 1:20 CDs-Cl,P/H2O2 (v/v), 0.5 mM KMnO4, 0.3 M H2O2, 0.04 M NaOH and 50.0 μM Cu2+ (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. S6b, the CL intensity decreased with increasing TA concentration. The linear relationship between the change CL signal (ΔI) and the logarithm of TA concentration was found to be in the range of 5.0 μM to100.0 μM (Fig. S6c). The linear regression equation was ΔI = 418.7 + 1160.1 lg[TA] (μM) (r = 0.9971) with a LOD of 0.14 µM.

To study the interference of other species to TA detection in the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4-Cu2+ system, some other substances with different concentration were added to the reaction system. Fig. S6d showed the method was free from interferences, that is, the TA detection method can achieve a high sensor response with good selectivity and stability）.

The determination of TA in wine simple was investigated using the standard addition method, and the results were shown in Table S2. The recoveries for detection of TA in wine samples ranged from 98.2% to 104.9%, while the RSD ranged from 1.04 % to 3.14%. The results indicated that the method can be used for the determination of TA in wine samples.

First, the effects of various active oxygen radical scavengers on the CL intensity of the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system were investigated (Table S1). As a classical reducing agent, ascorbic acid could be dehydrogenized by reactive oxygen species to form dehydroascorbic acid [44], which could further inhibit CL intensity by interacting with oxygen in the solution and forming diketogulonic acid. After addition of 1.0 mM ascorbic acid to the CL system, 50.4% inhibition of CL intensity was observed. This confirmed that the CL system was a radical reaction. Nitroblue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) is normally used for the detection of •O2 – radicals [45]. When 1.0 mM NBT was mixed with CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 solution, the CL signal decreased by 27.2%. Sodium azide (NaN3) is a physical quencher for 1O2 [46]. The generation of 1O2 in the studied system was confirmed by the observed quenching effect. The CL signal was 35.2% inhibited by 1.0 mM NaN3, which was an indication that 1O2 was involved in the CL reaction. The •OH is considered to be one of the most potent oxidizing agents. Thiourea is an effective radical scavenger for •OH [47]. In this CL system, an inhibition of 31.2% was achieved at a concentration of 1.0 mM thiourea, suggesting that •OH was released during the reaction.

Second, the FL spectra of CDs-Cl,P before and after the CL reaction were examined to determine the emission species. As shown in Fig. 4 a, it was found that the FL intensity of CDs-Cl,P decreased and FL emission wavelength did not change after the reaction, which indicated that some CDs-Cl,P disappeared during the CL reaction. It was suggested that some oxidizing free radicals generated during the CL reaction of H2O2-KMnO4 could consume some of the CDs-Cl,P, leading to a decrease in FL intensity of CDs-Cl,P. Meanwhile, the CL spectra were measured with high energy cutoff filters of different wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 4b, a peak at 535 nm was observed in the reaction of CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4, which was different from the CL spectrum of 1O2 or (1O2)2 [48]. Therefore, the enhancement of CDs-Cl,P on the H2O2-KMnO4 system could not be attributed to the catalytic mechanism. It was suggested that the CL peak at 535 nm might be due to the emission of CDs-Cl,P, which was identical to the FL spectra of CDs-Cl,P excited at 360 nm. The CL spectrum of CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 was red-shifted compared to the FL of CDs-Cl,P. The red shift most likely resulted from the lower energy spacings of the CDs-Cl,P surface states (for CL) compared to the energy for the most intense FL, which occurred mainly due to excitation and emission in the core of the CDs-Cl,P [49,50].

Based on the above results, the possible CL mechanism of the reaction of CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 was summarized. In the alkaline medium, the reaction between KMnO4 and H2O2 produced O2, which could accept an electron from CDs-Cl,P and form •O2 – [51]. The unstable •O2 – initiated the reactive oxygen chain reaction, which led to the formation of the reactive oxygen radical and 1O2. In addition, •OH could be obtained from H2O2 in alkaline solution [52], and CDs-Cl,P catalyzed the decomposition of H2O2 to form •OH and •O2 –, which led to the formation of 1O2 on its surface from the combination of these radicals. The •O2 – and 1O2 could attack each other and easily form a molecular pair (1O2)2 [51]. The reaction between KMnO4 and •O2 – promoted the formation of more reactive oxygen species [53]. Meanwhile, the •O2 − injected electron into the CDs-Cl,P to form negatively charged CDs-Cl,P•–, while •OH injected hole into the CDs-Cl,P form positively charged CDs-Cl,P•+ [54], and CDs-Cl,P* was formed through an electron-transfer annihilation between CDs-Cl,P•– and CDs-Cl,P•+. Therefore, enhanced CL emission occurred in the process of CDs-Cl,P* deexcitation.

It was known that there were several maximum wavelengths in the emission spectra of singlet oxygen, namely 1269, 762, 634, 476, and 381 nm, based on the transition from 1Δ, 1∑, 1Δ1Δ (molecular pair), 1Δ1∑ (molecular pair), 1∑1∑ (molecular pair) states, respectively [51]. In this way, energy transfer between (1O2)2 (donors) and CDs-Cl,P (acceptors) could occur because the broad emission band of (1O2)2 overlaps the absorption spectra of CDs-Cl,P [55], resulting in the CD-Cl,P* in the CDs-Cl,P-H2O2-KMnO4 system. A scheme for the possible CL mechanism was showed in Scheme 2 .

The aim of this work was to develop a new sensitized CL to extend the analytical application of the H2O2-KMnO4 system. Indeed, almost no CL signal was detected in the reaction between KMnO4 and H2O2 in alkaline medium. However, in the presence of CD-Cl,P, which acted as an efficient catalyst and energy acceptor for CL energy transfer, strong CL emission occurred. The emitting species in KMnO4-H2O2 system was 1O2 or (1O2)2 whereas CDs-Cl,P appeared as the emitting species in its presence. From a mechanistic point of view, the decomposition of H2O2 and the formation of 1O2 and excited (1O2)2 on the surface of CDs-Cl,P were promoted. In addition, the energy transfer and the contribution of the redox reaction of CDs-Cl,P to the formation of excited CDs-Cl,P supported the enhancement of CL signals. In view of our results, we proposed that the CL reaction of H2O2-KMnO4 proceeded via radical pathway in the presence of CDs-Cl,P and NaOH. The improved system was used to detect Cu2+ and TA with a low detection limit and a wide linear range. The obtained results have shown that the present method is more sensitive, faster, simpler and less expensive and can be used for the determination of Cu2+ and TA in real samples.
Yaqiong Yang: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yamei Jiang: Writing – review & editing. Xiaowei Wang: . Suqin Han: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 2013011013–3).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121434.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Wu, Novel chlorinated disinfection byproducts from tannic acid: nontargeted identification, formation pathways, and computationally predicted toxicity, J. Hazard.
